MEPZ SEZ Showcases 12 Years of Growth, Inclusion, and Sustainable Development Across the TAP Region
articles.full_content:
The MEPZ Special Economic Zone (MEPZ SEZ), operating under the Department of Commerce, Government of India, continues to play a pivotal role in driving exports, investment, employment generation, and inclusive development across the Tamil Nadu, Andaman & Nicobar Islands, and Puducherry (TAP) Region.
The TAP Region today hosts 50 operational Special Economic Zones—the highest in the country—out of a total of 62 SEZs, with 3 more set to become operational in the next three years. Comprising 749 operational SEZ units, alongside 288 Export Oriented Units (EOUs), the region has established itself as a global economic powerhouse.
Key Performance Highlights:
• FY 25-26 Exports: ₹ 2.22 Lakh Crore
• Cumulative Employment: 6.26 Lakh
• Cumulative Investment: ₹ 82,844 Crore
A Phenomenal Growth Trajectory
The TAP region has demonstrated remarkable and sustained economic momentum. Annual exports skyrocketed by 226.7%, rising from ₹ 68,104 crore in FY 2014-15 to a record ₹ 2.2 lakh crore in FY 2025-26.
This export boom is matched by robust capital inflow, with cumulative investments doubling from ₹ 41,489 crore to an impressive ₹ 82,844 crore, showcasing a 2x growth. Notably, this investment profile is heavily supported by a strong domestic foundation amounting to ₹ 74,165.75 crore, complemented by ₹ 8,677.9 crore in Foreign Direct Investment (FDI).
Employment generation remains a cornerstone of this success. The workforce has expanded by 118.5%, growing from 2.87 lakh to 6.27 lakh personnel—an addition of over 3.39 lakh jobs. The momentum continues, with 18,888 new jobs created in FY 2025-26 alone.
Sectoral Leaders & Major Investments
The region's export performance is anchored by the IT/ITES sector, which commands an 88.4% share of total exports, translating to approximately ₹ 1.9 lakh crore in FY 2025-26.
Beyond digital services, the manufacturing landscape is highly diversified. Engineering, Electronics & Hardware emerged as the leading manufacturing vertical, contributing 7.4% to the export basket with ₹ 16,384 crore.
Additionally, the Non-Leather Footwear sector demonstrated strong resilience and growth, increasing by 9.8% year-on-year from ₹ 3,669 crore to ₹ 4,027 crore.
This industrial engine is powered by critical zones such as MEPZ SEZ, ZOHO Estancia, World Trade Center, L&T Shipbuilding SEZ, DLF Cybercity IT SEZ, J Matadee FTWZ, and Cheyyar SEZ.

12 Years of Achievements of the Department of Commerce
articles.full_content:
Boosting Agri & Marine Exports From Odisha
Over the last twelve years, the Department of Commerce has played a crucial role in transforming India into a globally competitive trading nation. Inspired by the vision of Hon’ble Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi to transform India into a “Global Export Hub,” the Department of Commerce has promoted the spirit of “Vocal for Local” and “Local to Global,” enabling Indian products and businesses to expand their presence across global markets. Through policy reforms, digital transformation, trade facilitation, and strategic trade partnerships, the Department has strengthened India’s position as a major player in global commerce and supported the vision of making India a “Global Export Hub.”
India’s exports recorded remarkable growth from FY 2014-15 to FY 2025-26. Total exports increased from US$ 468 billion to a historic high of US$ 863 billion, reflecting a CAGR of 5.7%. Merchandise exports rose from US$ 310 billion to US$ 442 billion, while services exports grew from US$ 158 billion to over US$ 421 billion, achieving a CAGR of 9.3%. Engineering goods, electronic goods, pharmaceuticals, textiles, agricultural products, marine products, and gems and jewellery emerged as major export drivers.
The Department introduced several digital initiatives to improve ease of doing business, including the Trade Connect Portal, Trade Intelligence and Analytics (TIA) Portal, Merchandise Imports Monitoring Portal (MIMP), e-IEC generation, and online certification systems. These measures enhanced transparency, reduced compliance burdens, and improved access to trade-related services.
India also deepened trade relations through landmark agreements such as the India-UAE CEPA, India-Australia ECTA, India-UK FTA, India-EU FTA, India-Oman CEPA, and India-New Zealand FTA. These agreements expanded market access, reduced tariffs, promoted investment, and strengthened India’s integration with global value chains.
A notable success story has been the growth of India’s marine products sector. Seafood exports reached a record value of USD 8.45 billion in 2025-26, with frozen shrimp accounting for more than 66% of export earnings. The United States and China remained the largest importers of Indian seafood. India’s marine exports have been supported by initiatives promoting quality standards, traceability, infrastructure development, and market diversification.
Odisha has emerged as one of India’s most promising seafood-exporting states. With a long coastline, favourable aquaculture conditions, and strong fisheries infrastructure, Odisha exported 100,897 metric tonnes of seafood valued at USD 623.28 million (₹5,428.67 crore) during 2025 26. The state recorded growth of 9.63% in export quantity and 11.90% in export value over the previous year. Odisha has 64 registered seafood exporters, 45 processing plants, modern cold storage facilities, and 36 shrimp hatcheries supporting aquaculture development. Vannamei shrimp production reached 5

Inspired by the vision of Hon’ble Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi to transform India into a “Global Export Hub,” the Department of Commerce has promoted the spirit of “Vocal for Local” and “Local to Global,” enabling Indian products and businesses to expand their presence across global markets. Through policy reforms, digital transformation, trade facilitation, and strategic trade partnerships, the Department has strengthened India’s position as a major player in global commerce and supported the vision of making India a “Global Export Hub.” India’s exports recorded remarkable growth from FY 2014-15 to FY 2025-26.

Shri Ujjwal Kumar Ghosh, Joint Secretary, Department of Commerce, Interacts with Exporters in Kochi; Highlights 12 Years of Growth in India’s Agricultural Exports and Emerging Opportunities through FTAs
articles.full_content:
Kochi : 10 June 2026
Shri Ujjwal Kumar Ghosh, IAS, Joint Secretary, Department of Commerce, Government of India, held an interactive meeting with exporters from multiple sectors, including agriculture and marine products, as well as representatives of Export Promotion Bodies, in Kochi, Kerala on 10 June 2026. The meeting was held under the theme “Indian Agricultural Exports: A Journey of Scale, Strength and Success — 12 Years of Growth, Resilience and Global Reach.”
Shri Ghosh highlighted the impressive progress achieved in India’s agricultural exports over the past 12 years, noting an increase from USD 32.08 billion in FY 2014–15 to a record USD 52.55 billion in FY 2025–26.
He observed that recent and ongoing Free Trade Agreements (FTAs) with the United Kingdom, the European Union, New Zealand, Oman, Australia, the UAE and the EFTA countries are opening significant avenues for further expansion and diversification of India’s agricultural export basket.
Improved market access under these FTAs is expected to create fresh opportunities across several segments, notably marine products (including shrimp and value-added seafood); Basmati and premium non-Basmati rice; spices; nutraceutical ingredients and traditional food products; tea, especially orthodox and specialty varieties; fruits and vegetables; millet-based products; meat; organic products and processed foods.
Shri Ghosh reiterated that the Department of Commerce remains committed to enabling Indian exporters to fully capitalize on the opportunities presented by FTAs and other export-promotion initiative.

The meeting was held under the theme “Indian Agricultural Exports: A Journey of Scale, Strength and Success — 12 Years of Growth, Resilience and Global Reach.” Shri Ghosh highlighted the impressive progress achieved in India’s agricultural exports over the past 12 years, noting an increase from USD 32.08 billion in FY 2014–15 to a record USD 52.55 billion in FY 2025–26. He observed that recent and ongoing Free Trade Agreements (FTAs) with the United Kingdom, the European Union, New Zealand, Oman, Australia, the UAE and the EFTA countries are opening significant avenues for further expansion and diversification of India’s agricultural export basket.

Commerce Secretary Urges Enhanced Trade, Investment and Business Engagement at India–Tajikistan Joint Commission Meeting
articles.full_content:
India and Tajikistan agree to deepen trade, investment, pharmaceuticals, agriculture, services, energy, connectivity and capacity-building cooperation India offers to work with Tajikistan for expanding procurement of high-quality and affordable Indian pharmaceutical products
Commerce Secretary, Government of India, Shri Rajesh Agrawal, delivered the opening remarks at the 12th Meeting of the India-Tajikistan Joint Commission on Trade, Economic, Scientific and Technical Cooperation, held today through video conference. He noted that the Joint Commission was meeting at a time when the global trade, supply-chain and technology landscape had changed significantly, and underlined the need for India and Tajikistan to convert political goodwill and historical friendship into stronger economic outcomes through greater focus on trade, investment, market access, regulatory cooperation and business engagement.
The meeting was co-chaired by Shri Mohit Yadav, Joint Secretary, Department of Commerce, Government of India, and H.E. Mr. Nuriddinzoda Ahliddin Nuriddin, Deputy Minister of Economic Development and Trade of the Republic of Tajikistan. Senior officials and representatives from relevant ministries, departments and agencies of both countries participated in the meeting.
The meeting was held in a warm and constructive atmosphere, reflecting the long-standing friendship, civilizational bonds and mutual trust between India and Tajikistan. Both sides reviewed the progress made since the 11th Session of the Joint Commission and exchanged views on further strengthening bilateral economic engagement.
The Indian side further noted India’s strong economic growth and export achievements during the last 12 years under the visionary leadership of Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi. The Indian side also highlighted India’s strong economic momentum. India recorded GDP growth of 7.7 percent in FY 2025-26 and 7.8 percent in Q4 of FY 2025-26, reflecting the strength of reforms, enterprise and public effort. India’s total exports during FY 2025-26 were estimated at US$ 863 billion, including merchandise exports of USD 442 billion and services exports of US$ 421 billion.
The Indian side noted the positive momentum in bilateral merchandise trade. India’s exports to Tajikistan are estimated at US$ 58.12 million in 2025-26, rising 27.23% compared to previous year. Drug formulations and biologicals was the major product of India’s exports to Tajikistan during this period, followed by pulses, industrial machinery for dairy and related sectors, medical and scientific instruments, tea, Ayush and herbal products, and refrigeration machinery. This reflects rising opportunities for farmers, Indian MSMEs and businesses.
Both sides reviewed regulatory and market access issues affecting bilateral trade and agreed to strengthen coordination between competent authorities, trade bodies and business chambers. They emphasized the need for regular interaction to address operational concerns
